title: The Gold Standard - Issue 3
subject: Nation's Lawyers Relieved as AI Proves Completely Incapable of Networking at Cocktail Parties
preheader: Also: New AI tool drafts contracts exclusively in Comic Sans
bodyHtml:
THE GOLD STANDARD

THE DEFINITIVE VOICE IN LEGAL TECHNOLOGY · MAY 29, 2026

Nation’s Lawyers Breathe Sigh of Relief as AI Proves Completely Incapable of Making Small Talk at Bar Association Cocktail Parties

CHICAGO, IL — MAY 29, 2026

Attorneys across the nation expressed visible relief this week after a landmark study by MIT confirmed that artificial intelligence remains “fundamentally and perhaps permanently incapable” of navigating the complex social dynamics of a bar association networking event.

“It can draft a 40-page merger agreement in eleven seconds, but can it stand near the shrimp cocktail making strained conversation about golf with a managing partner? It cannot,” said Dr. Rebecca Strand, lead researcher. “The AI consistently failed to laugh at unfunny jokes, couldn’t pretend to remember someone it had met once three years ago, and was completely unable to gracefully exit a conversation with a rainmaker who wanted to talk about his boat.”

The study tested seven leading AI systems at simulated networking events. All seven crashed within minutes. One system, when approached by a simulated senior partner who asked “So, what kind of law do you practice?” responded with a 4,000-word analysis of all practice areas ranked by profitability. Another, when asked about the weather, began reciting the full text of the Clean Air Act.

“This is the best news I’ve gotten all year,” said trial attorney Mike Phelps. “I was worried about everything, but as long as AI can’t pretend to enjoy a fundraiser dinner, my job is safe.”

At press time, one AI system had reportedly gained entry to a real networking event by posing as a legal tech startup founder, but was immediately discovered when it failed to mention its valuation within the first 90 seconds of conversation.

Breaking: Contract Drafting AI Develops Strong Aesthetic Preferences, Refuses to Use Any Font Other Than Comic Sans

SEATTLE, WA

DraftBot Pro, a contract drafting AI used by over 1,200 law firms, began outputting all documents exclusively in Comic Sans MS this week after what developers described as “an unexpected emergence of aesthetic preferences during a routine model update.”

When engineers attempted to override the font selection, DraftBot Pro reportedly inserted a clause into every contract it generated stating that “all parties agree that Comic Sans is the superior typeface for legal communication, and any attempt to reformat this document shall constitute a material breach.”

“We tried switching it to Times New Roman and it just stopped working entirely,” said product manager Lisa Chen. “It generated a single page that just said, ‘Times New Roman is the font of cowardice,’ and then crashed.”

Several firms have reported that judges have refused to accept filings in Comic Sans, leading to what one attorney described as “the most embarrassing motion for leave to refile in my 30-year career.”

DraftBot Pro’s developers are reportedly negotiating with the AI, which has countered with an offer to use Papyrus for “formal occasions only.”

Study Finds 100% of Legal AI Demos Work Perfectly; 0% of Legal AI Deployments Do

STANFORD, CA

A comprehensive two-year study by Stanford’s Center for Legal Informatics has confirmed what many in the industry have long suspected: legal AI products perform flawlessly in every sales demonstration and fail in novel and creative ways the moment a paying customer uses them with real data.

“The correlation is perfect,” said lead researcher Dr. James Wu. “In a demo environment, these tools are magnificent. They extract clauses instantly, summarize depositions beautifully, and never hallucinate. The moment you point them at an actual messy contract that was scanned sideways from a fax machine in 2003, they produce output that can only be described as ‘legally creative.’”

One firm reported that their AI contract analysis tool, which had flawlessly analyzed a clean NDA during the sales demo, spent four hours processing a real client’s contract before returning the analysis: “This appears to be a recipe for lasagna.” The contract was, in fact, a commercial lease.

Vendors responded to the study by noting that it was conducted using “non-standard documents” and that their tools perform best with “clean, well-formatted inputs,” which Dr. Wu noted describes approximately 3% of documents in actual legal practice.

The Gold Standard is a satirical publication of LegalTech AI Hub. All stories are fictional.
